nativeRange.habitat: Shallow bogs, marshes, pond and stream margins; emerges from wet mud and shallow standing water

decorPreferences: strong overhead lighting for compact carpeting

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, large cichlids, or silver dollars

summary: Whorled Pennywort is a creeping aquatic plant with distinctive round, umbrella-shaped leaves arranged in whorls along trailing runners. It generally favors strong light and added CO2 to stay compact and low — without those conditions it tends to grow tall and leggy.
careNotes: Whorled Pennywort (Hydrocotyle verticillata) is a creeping aquatic plant native to North and South America and the Caribbean, recognizable by its round, half-dollar-shaped leaves arranged in whorls along trailing runners. It is worth knowing that several other Hydrocotyle species appear in the aquarium trade under similar names: H. tripartita is the much more compact 'Japan' or 'Mini' Hydrocotyle, H. leucocephala is the loosely upright Brazilian Pennywort, and H. sibthorpioides is another compact creeper sold simply as 'Pennywort'. Whorled Pennywort is distinguished from these by its strictly trailing growth and its larger, clearly rounded leaf shape. It is generally considered an advanced plant to keep well. In low-to-medium light it tends to grow tall and leggy, quickly losing the compact carpeting form many aquarists are after. Strong lighting, added CO2, and a nutrient-rich substrate are commonly advised to keep it dense and low — under those conditions it has been reported to spread rapidly as a foreground or midground creeper, and frequent trimming is generally needed to maintain the carpet. The plant grows readily both submerged and emersed and can also be cultivated as a marginal or pond plant; it is sometimes floated at the surface in the short term.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via vegetative means: separate runners or daughter plants from a healthy mother plant and replant them, or cut the creeping stem and replant individual segments. Splitting dense clumps is also commonly used. The plant produces new shoots along its trailing stem that can be trimmed off anywhere and replanted.
